mnemonics.title: Precedence Order Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: Remember precedence from highest to lowest: PUMAS-RBELCOA (Postfix Unary Multiplicative Additive Shift Relational Bitwise Equality Logical Conditional Assignment). Or simpler: Think of it as: Postfix → Unary → Arithmetic → Shift → Relational → Equality → Bitwise → Logical → Assignment.

mnemonics.title: Left-to-Right Rule
mnemonics.content: Most operators are LEFT-associative. Only THREE are RIGHT: Unary, Ternary, Assignment. Remember: UTA = Unary Ternary Assignment = Right to Left.

acronyms.usage: Top-level precedence categories in order from highest to lowest

acronyms.expansion: Relational Bitwise Equality Logical Conditional Assignment

acronyms.usage: The three right-to-left associative operators in Java

visualTips.visualization: Think of operator precedence as a pyramid: Postfix at top (highest), then Unary, then Arithmetic (*/%), then Shift, then Relational, then Bitwise, then Logical (&& ||), then Assignment at bottom (lowest).
Draw arrows: Most operators have ← (left arrow) indicating left-to-right evaluation. Assignment, Ternary, and Unary have → (right arrow) indicating right-to-left evaluation.

analogies.analogy: Operator precedence in Java is like math order of operations (PEMDAS) - just with Java-specific operators. Just as multiplication comes before addition, Java evaluates * before +.

analogies.anal analogy: Think of operators as people in a priority queue: Postfix jumps to the front, Unary pushes ahead, Arithmetic waits in the middle, Assignment stands at the end. They get served in that order.

analogies.analogy: Operators are like family members: Postfix is the youngest (served first), Unary is the parent, Arithmetic are the children, Bitwise are cousins, Logical are distant relatives, and Assignment is the eldest (served last).
